prior.content_md:
*Note: This section replaces the Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives section (Section 13) of the standard Regulatory Fact Document format, as the most material content identified for this item is regulatory intersection rather than independent research findings. No publicly available research specific to the workspace's cargo verticals and FuelEU Maritime cost pass-through was identified in the available source pool.*

**Intersection with EU ETS (Directive 2003/87/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2023/959).** EU ETS for shipping entered its phase-in period in 2024 (40% of verified emissions surrendered), reaching 100% in 2026. ETS and FuelEU Maritime are additive cost layers on the same voyage: ETS surcharges cover the cost of allowance surrender per tonne of CO₂ emitted; FuelEU surcharges cover the carrier's penalty or fuel cost for meeting the GHG intensity limit. A vessel on a Rotterdam–Hamburg feeder that burns conventional fuel will generate both an ETS surcharge and a FuelEU surcharge on the same leg. The workspace must ensure its invoice audit framework distinguishes these two surcharges and does not conflate them.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Recital 9; Directive 2003/87/EC as amended by Directive (EU) 2023/959; OJ L 234, 22.9.2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng*

**Intersection with AFIR (Regulation (EU) 2023/1804).** The OPS obligation in FuelEU Maritime Article 6 is directly paired with the port-side OPS infrastructure deployment obligation in AFIR Article 9. Carriers can only comply with the OPS connection obligation if ports have installed the infrastructure. Port OPS deployment timelines under AFIR directly affect the risk of carrier non-compliance and associated surcharge exposure for the workspace. EU core network ports must have OPS for containerships and passenger ships by 2030 under AFIR; smaller ports on the comprehensive network by 2035. Ports not meeting AFIR deadlines create OPS exemption conditions for carriers under FuelEU Maritime Article 6(5)(c), reducing OPS-related surcharge exposure on those port calls.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Articles 6, 7; Regulation (EU) 2023/1804, Article 9; OJ L 234, 22.9.2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng; https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng*

**Intersection with proposed transport emissions regulation (COM(2023) 441 final).** The Commission's proposal for harmonised measurement of transport and logistics GHG emissions would, if adopted, require emissions data to flow through the logistics chain from carriers to freight forwarders to shippers. FuelEU Maritime compliance data (well-to-wake GHG intensity per vessel) would be an input into the ocean leg emissions calculation under that framework. The workspace should track this proposal's legislative progress; if adopted, it elevates FuelEU data collection from a commercial best practice to a regulated data intermediary obligation.

*Source: COM(2023) 441 final; European Parliament Legislative Train Schedule (file: measurement-of-transport-and-logistics-emissions). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=comnat:COM_2023_0441_FIN; https://www.europarl.europa.eu/legislative-train/spotlight-JD%2023-24/file-measurement-of-transport-and-logistics-emissions*

---
